coredata.dc:title: Recent advances in chemiluminescence based on carbonaceous dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Advances in Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Shah, Syed Niaz Ali
Lin, Jin-Ming
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a broad overview concerning the most recent progress of carbon dots (CDs) in chemiluminescence (CL) as well as the mechanisms and applications are presented. CDs have excellent optical and electronic properties and are very important advancement in the fast growing domain of nanotechnology. CDs enhance the ultraweak CL of different systems. The mechanisms and applications of these enhanced CL reactions are discussed. It is worthy to note that CDs participate in CL reactions as catalysts, energy acceptors or are directly involved in redox reactions with radicals in CL systems. Sometimes, these processes taking place simultaneously to enhance CL intensity. In this report, recent advances in CD based CL are comprehensively summarized and their applications in detection of various reagents and biological molecules are reviewed. The challenges and future prospects of this field are also discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chemiluminescence
Enhancement mechanism
Applications
Review

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Advances in Colloid and Interface Science

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CL mechanism for CTAB@CDs amplified Co2+ ultraweak CL emissions.

ce:figure.ce:source: (Reproduced with permission from [48], copyright 2014, American Chemical Society.)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: CL reaction mechanism for CDs enhanced H2O2–NaHSO3 system [6].
Schematic illustration of the CL mechanism of CDsNaNO2 H2O2 system [26].
Schematic illustration for the CL mechanism of CDsNaNO2 H2O2 Na2CO3 system [49].
Schematic illustration of CL mechanism of CDs-N-bromosuccinimide-luminol system [50].

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the CL mechanism of NaIO4 H2O2 CDs system.

ce:figure.ce:source: (Reproduced with permission from [3], copyright 2012, Royal society of Chemistry.)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The mechanism of enhancement for the CL of KIO4 H2O2 system by NCDs and the decreasing and increasing effect by acids and base, respectively [52].
Possible mechanism of CDs-DBS-LDH nanocatalyst for the decomposition of acidified H2O2 to produce abundant •OH radicals [13].
The proposed mechanism for Luminol–KMnO4–CDs CL mechanism, adapted after permission from [58], copyright 2015, Royal Society of Chemistry.
Schematic illustration of FL and CL mechanism in CDs–KMnO4 and CDs–cerium(IV) systems. CL1 and CL2 represent two CL routes in the system [12].
Schematic illustration for CL mechanism of CDs-KMnO4 and r-CDs-KMnO4 systems. Adapted with permission from [63], copyright 2014, Elsevier.
Mechanism of the enhancement of different systems by CDs.

row.entry: OH, NH2, NH3 + & CO on surface of CDs
Surface group of CDs are responsible for CL
Surface structure of CDs are critical for CL enhancement
The excellent electron donating property of CDs resulted in CL
Classical oxidants causes CL from CDs
unique structure of CDs-DBS-LDH was responsible for the efficient catalysis
Formation of TEA free radical causes enhanced CL
The electron injection into CDs and the electron–hole annihilation resulted in CL
Energy Transfer & redox reaction takes place
COOH and OH groups passivated CNSs
surface chemistry & the presence of surface states are responsible for CL
CTAB bilayer confined at the interface of CDs amplified the CL signals
Energy Transfer & redox reaction takes place
Electrostatic interaction between CDs & luminol anion
rCDs in the alkaline solution interact with N-bromosuccinimide
Catalysis, CRET & electron hole annihilation process
Catalysis, Energy Transfer & redox reaction takes place
Direct CL and CRET are responsible for high CL
The catalytic activity of CDs are responsible for CL
Various surface energy traps existing on g-CNQDs are responsible for CL
ClO− directly oxidizes GQDs to elicit CL
The strong oxidizing ability of Ni(IV) causes intense CL
Catalysis and electron transfer from CDs results in high CL
Radiative recombination of oxidant injected holes and electrons
Electron transfer from g-CNQDs to K3[Fe(CN)6] could inject hole into the g-CNQDs.
GQDs facilitates the oxidation of Rhodamine B and accelerates the generation of CL emitting species
Surface group of CDs are responsible for CL
CDs function to reduce the generated Ru(bpy)3 3+ to excited state
CRET & direct oxidation of CDs causes enhanced CL
BPEI-CDs can generate CL signals in alkaline solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of CDs enhanced CL system in Analytical fields.

row.entry: TEA concentration is directly proportional to CL signals
Linear relation between CL intensity and nitrite conc.
CL intensity have linear relation with H2O2 conc.
Inhibition resulted from the reductive consumption of oxidant
Linear relation between relative CL intensity and Co(II) conc.
N-bromosuccinimide converts Co2+ to Co3+ in the alkaline environment
Competition of analytes with N-CDs for •O2 – radicals in the system
Reducing property of BPA may compete with CDs & leads to decrease CL
Strongly oxidizing NaClO affecting g-CNQDs surface states induced CL
CL intensity have linear relation with ClO− conc.
Competitive reaction of analytes with Ni(IV)
Due to strong reducibility of m-phenylenediamine, it consumes KMnO4 in system
Reducing groups of analytes compete with CDs for active oxygen intermediates
Formation of non-luminescent complex between g-CNQDs and Hg(II) ion
CL inhibition of DA was based on competition between DA and g-CNQDs for K3[Fe(CN)6]
2,4-DCP has a reducing property and compete with RhB for Ce(IV) which leads to a decrease in the CL
Nitrophenol reduces the generated Ru(bpy)3 3+ to its excited state, which leads to an improvement in the CL
Dopamine has a reducing property and compete with CDs and thiosulfate for Ce(IV)
Fe3+ was selectively captured by BPEI and injected holes into the CDs

head.ce:title: Recent advances in chemiluminescence based on carbonaceous dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Microanalytical Methods and Instrumentation, The Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Microanalytical Methods and Instrumentation
The Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a broad overview concerning the most recent progress of carbon dots (CDs) in chemiluminescence (CL) as well as the mechanisms and applications are presented. CDs have excellent optical and electronic properties and are very important advancement in the fast growing domain of nanotechnology. CDs enhance the ultraweak CL of different systems. The mechanisms and applications of these enhanced CL reactions are discussed. It is worthy to note that CDs participate in CL reactions as catalysts, energy acceptors or are directly involved in redox reactions with radicals in CL systems. Sometimes, these processes taking place simultaneously to enhance CL intensity. In this report, recent advances in CD based CL are comprehensively summarized and their applications in detection of various reagents and biological molecules are reviewed. The challenges and future prospects of this field are also discussed.

Scheme 1 carbon dots enhanced chemiluminescence.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon is one of the most abundant natural elements and is present in a variety of valuable materials including nanomaterials (NMs). In particular, carbon based dots; including carbon dots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [1,2] are new investigated class of ‘zero-dimensional’ NMs [3]. Generally, they have also been referred as carbogenic nanodots due to their oxygen content [4–6]. In fact, transition from GQDs to CDs transpires by controlling layers of graphene [1]. Especially, CDs have size below 10nm and are in spherical shape and displaying size and excitation (λex) dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior [4–7]. Shortly after the discovery of CDs through preparative electrophoresis [8], the development and application of this material fascinated scientific community [9]. Indeed, CDs have gradually become a rising star of carbon based NMs due to their benign and inexpensive nature [10–13] and attracted considerable attention as nascent QDs. Typically, CDs contain many carboxylic acid functional groups at their surface, assigning them excellent water solubility, facile functionalization [14,15] and strong luminescence [11]. In contrast to conventional semiconductor QDs and organic dyes, CDs have advantages of low toxicity, high chemical stability, high water solubility, biocompatibility, facile modification, high resistance to photo-bleaching [16–19], easy preparation and environmental friendliness. These properties make CDs superior to QDs and magnet of significant research [4,20]. Thus CDs appear as alternative to QDs in many areas and have induced much attention from great number of researchers [16,21,22].

Moreover, CDs are interesting newcomers as fascinating luminescent materials and promising building blocks for future nano-devices in the world of NMs. In that sense, the outstanding electronic properties of CDs as excellent electron donors and acceptors [4]; grant them wide potentials in optronics, catalysis and sensors [11]. Research studies show that CDs have an electron-donating property [23]; however, this property manifests itself only under an external driving force such as photo- and electronic induction. In contrast, Dou et al. found the generation of •O2 – in strong alkaline medium by electron transfer from CDs through chemiluminescence (CL) method [10]. Similarly, Zhao et al. also reported the same result [24]. This provided direct evidence that CDs also exhibited electron-donating property without any external force. Unfortunately, CDs always suffer from low quantum yields which severely limit its practical applications [3,25]. In this situation, doping is a commonly used methodology to tune PL possessions of materials. Generally, various doping methods with lots of elements such as N, S and P have been reported to tune the properties of CDs. Nitrogen-doping is the most studied way to enhance the emission of CDs, possibly by an upward shift in the Fermi level and electrons in the conduction band [11]. Thus N-doped materials have attracted considerable attention recently, owing to their higher quantum yield and ability to be internalized by cells [19]. In past several years, most research has been concentrated on syntheses and properties of CDs. Recently, some efforts were made for analytical applications of CDs based on their CL properties. Our research group was the first to find CL possessions of CDs in ONOOH system [26].

In sight of its ultimate significance and diversity of concrete applications, CL has been a striking area of intensive research for over 30years [27]. CL is the formation of electromagnetic radiation through chemical reaction. During chemical reaction, intermediate radicals form unstable products which decompose further to produce electronically excited species. These highly excited species deactivate via CL emission [12,28–30]. CL in aqueous systems is caused mainly by redox reactions [30]. In general, chemical reaction generates energy in sufficient amounts (>41kcal/mol) [31,32] to provoke transition of an electron from its ground state to its excited state. The excited species deactivate to lose its energy by undergoing different process i.e. chemical reactions, collisional deactivation, internal conversion or inter-system crossing. Moreover, electronic transition is often complemented by vibrational and rotational changes [33]. The excited species either deactivate to ground state or decompose through energy transfer to luminophores of relatively high quantum yield [32].

CL is becoming a promising technique with increased reliability, fast response, cost efficient instrumentation and simple operation [34]. Additionally, CL is often designated as dark-field technique due to its inherent low background signal which leads to improved detection limits [32,35] and has been proven as a sensitive and facile analytical method [35,36]. As a result, CL has been widely applied in clinical diagnosis, environmental monitoring and food safety at ultra-trace levels [30,37–39]. Besides this, it was easily operated and automated when combined with flow-injection analysis (FIA) [40] or high performance liquid chromatography [41]. Being an exciting research area, CL has been explored for many years as an effective tool for gaining insight into chemical reactions and allied applications [42].

CL processes can be divided into two types i.e. direct and indirect or sensitized CL [27,31]. The direct CL takes place by direct oxidation of target compound to produce emitting species. In contrary, indirect CL occurs by the enhancing effect of certain luminescent compounds [28]. Generally, direct CL proceeds through two steps. The first step comprises production of excited molecule and is of chemical nature (reaction 1) [43].
(1)
A + B → P ∗ + C
P ∗
excited
product

In contrast, second step is of physical nature and comprises several routes (e.g. emission of light, energy transfer or quenching). In this step excited molecule switch off to its ground state (reaction 2).
(2)
P ∗ → P + hv

Sometimes, excited product (P*) results in an inoperable emission but it can handover its energy to a proficient fluorophore (F) (reaction 3).
(3)
P ∗ + F → P + F ∗
(4)
F ∗ → F + hv

In this case, emission is of high yield and is enhanced and can label as indirect CL (enhanced or sensitized) (reaction 4) [43]. In particular, energy transfer involves the excitation of a molecule that passes its energy to other molecules. The loss of excitation energy results in quenching of donor and may result in luminescence from energy acceptor. In fact, in resonance or dipole mechanism, the donor and acceptor species are not in contact with one another and may as far as 10nm (1nm is more common). In classical sense, the energy donor molecule may be thought of as the transmitting antenna that creates an electrical field in its vicinity while the potential acceptor within the range of this electrical field functions as the receiving antennae and absorbs energy from the field resulting in their electronic excitation.

However, development of some CL systems was limited because the intensity was not strong enough as demand by detection requirements [38,39]. In addition, 40–70kcal/mol is required for visible emission (400–750nm), while only certain redox reactions generate this much energy [44]. Likewise, emission from CL reactions of inorganic molecules is relatively weak due to low quantum yield. Therefore, it is needed to improve CL intensity for analytical uses [28,31]. Often, catalysts are employed in a CL reaction to make light emitting reactions processed at a convenient rate. The catalyst is usually a co-oxidant; that is, it assists electron transfer [44]. For this purpose, reaction strategies (i.e. catalytic process, energy transfer or redox reactions) or compounds (NMs or transition elements) have been proposed [38,39,45]. The introduction of NMs brought a broad prospect for application of CL methods [38,39]. In that sense, CDs with unique catalytic possessions have grown increasing interest recently as an alternative to catalyze redox CL reactions of different systems under appropriate circumstances [36]. With the application of CDs in various systems, significant breakthroughs in CL studies have been observed. Furthermore, CL mechanism and prospective applications of ultraweak CL systems have gradually gained great research attention [37] in recent past.

CL techniques are suitable for quantitative analysis as the reaction rate depends on chemical concentration. The practicality of CL system in analytical chemistry is based on some distinct characteristic [44].
(1)
CL method simultaneously embraces luminescence and kinetic features. It grants high sensitivity, excellent detection limits and wide dynamic ranges.
(2)
No external light source is required and has simple instrumentation. Furthermore, CL presents some benefits such as lack of background signal, problems related to external source and interferences.
(3)
Linearity and selectivity are mostly reliant on reaction conditions.
(4)
The technique is useful for analysis of a wide variety of compounds which take part in CL process such as CL precursors, catalyst or inhibitors and fluorophores.
(5)
CL reactions can be tied as detection tool in chromatography, capillary electrophoresis or immunoassay, providing quantitative and/or qualitative information of large range of compounds.
(6)
Depending on the nature of CL reaction and analyte, the increase or decrease in CL intensity will be directly associated to analyte concentration.

Recently, CDs have been given great attention in many fields and nanotechnology has gradually introduced these NMs in analytical science mostly as chemical sensors. They have excellent solubility in water which significantly expands their uses [36]. Obviously, in past decade research in CL was focused on scrutiny of nanosized redox catalysts to engender enhanced CL signals. These fascinating CL assets of CDs may offer new prospects possibly for analytical uses besides mechanistic elucidation. Even though processes for CL enhancement are attained through various reaction strategies (e.g. catalytic, energy transfer or redox reactions) (Table 1 ), only a small number of these studies have been used for analytical determinations. Generally, analytes can either inhibit or enhance CDs enhanced CL signals, offering the base for growth of a number of sensitive approaches of analysis [31]. Therefore, CL can be pertained to the fortitude of various CL related species [37]. In spite the fact that CDs intensified CL reaction strategies open new prospects in modern analytical science, huge magnification of CL signal, specificity and selectivity remains a problem [31]. So far, specificity and selectivity in CDs enhanced assay were attained through labeling using biomolecule-functionalized CDs.

There are several reviews about the importance of CL methods in analytical chemistry and their applications in determining great variety of compounds including the applications in food analysis [32] and biomedical applications [1]. In addition, our group published a review on advances and applications of CL immunoassay in clinical diagnosis and foods safety in 2015 [46], and recently published another review on “Graphene materials-based chemiluminescence for sensing” [47], but no review is present in literature regarding our present topic. In order to summarize and compare the uses of CDs in different CL systems, distinctive characteristics, analytical parameters and applications of these CL systems are listed here. As from Table 2 one can observe that different compounds could be directly or indirectly quantified. Herein, we drew the interest with CDs enhanced CL systems for recognition of different analytes. The CL technique are often more selective and sensitive than spectrophotometric or fluorimetric practices. In fact, the growing demands for very sensitive and selective analyses in many areas of analytical sciences require CL systems to deliver significant and fascinating outcomes. Herein, we discussed the analytical efficacy of catalytic effect of CDs enhanced CL systems. The fact that CL intensity can be enhanced or inhibited by numerous analytes was used for emergent CL means of analysis.

For obvious mechanism, we sought to comprehend the essence of CDs-intensified CL mechanisms by deliberating different CL systems. We are going to cite some CL systems enhanced by CDs and their probable mechanisms published in recent years. The different systems are explained in the following headings and the results are summarized in Table 1.

CTAB@CDs enhanced the CL from Co2+ H2O2 OH– system as reported by Shi et al. [14]. They found that CTAB@CDs acted as a catalyst decomposing hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to generate •OH which resulted in (O2)2*. Conclusively, enhanced CL emission from CTAB@CDs was attributed as energy transfer from (O2)2* instead of electron-transfer annihilation with the help of PL studies of CDs before and after the CL reaction. Additionally, maximum emission wavelength and PL passion of CTAB@CDs remained unaffected in Co2+ H2O2 OH– system, specifying that the structure of CDs was not demolished after the CL reaction. Moreover, a highly selective Co2+ sensing system based on CL resonance energy transfer between Fenton-like reaction and CDs-modulated microenvironment (i.e., necklace model) was developed. The proposed system exhibited a stable response to Co2+ over a concentration range of 1.0–1000nM with 0.67nM LOD and was successfully applied to Co2+ detection in HepG2 cells. In this report it has not shown that why the CL signals in presence of Co2+ is higher than the other metal ions.

By employing the same principle, Dong et al. also reported that CTAB-CDs enhanced H2O2 prompted ultraweak CL systems, such as Co2+ prompted Fenton like reaction, peroxynitrous acid (ONOOH) system and peroxymonocarbonate (HCO4 −) system [48]. Shortly, the effect of CTAB-CDs on Co2+ H2O2 OH– reaction was studied and found a substantial increase in CL emission of the system. In fact, the CL efficacy of CTAB-CDs with their analogues designated that CTAB bilayer cramped in CDs could perform as a distinct micelle microenvironment, serving the entrance of reactive intermediates to the central carbon core (Fig. 1 ). The surfactant microenvironments as distinctive CL reaction means can solubilize, organize and concentrate reactants, thus have established ample interest in intensifying CL emission in solution based methods. The analytical performance of suggested CL process was examined for Co2+ sensing. There was very good linear relationship in relative CL strength and Co2+ concentration in range of 0.1–500nM with 0.9945 correlation coefficient. The RSD values for 0.1μM Co2+ was 1.6% (n =11) and LOD was 0.07nM (S/N=3). Unfortunately, they haven't checked the effect of other metal ions on the CL behavior of this system.

The CL intensity from NaHSO3 H2O2 system was considerably improved by CDs as explored by our group [6]. The enhancement was attributed to excited CDs* which were produced by three possible ways. Firstly, CDs acted as catalyst and facilitated radical generation. The CL emitters e.g. SO2* and 1O2 was presumed to be generated through simultaneous practices of decomposition and radical recombination (reactions 5-8). Secondly, SO2* and 1O2 relocated energies to CDs which operated as emitters in improved CL. Lastly, the intermediate radicals spawned in H2O2-NaHSO3 reaction for example •OH, •SO4 −, •O2 – and •SO3 − radicals reacted with CDs to produce positively (CD•+) and negatively (CD•−) charged CDs. Since, excited CD* were produced through electron-transfer annihilation effect. The boosted CL emission ensued when CD* resumed to ground-state (Fig. 2 ). The mechanism of CL enhancement of this system was well elaborated but no practical utility was checked.
(5)
HOSO 2 −
+
H 2
O 2
→
HOOSO 2 −
+
H 2
O
(6)
HOOSO 2 −
→
S
•
O 3 −
+
O
•
H
(7)
O
•
H +
HSO 3 −
→
S
•
O 3 −
+
H 2
O
(8)
2
S
•
O 3 −
→
S 2
O 6
2 −
→
SO 2 ∗
+
SO 4
2 −

Whereas, Zhou et al. applied the inhibited CL system of CDs sensitized PHSA CL scheme for trace analysis of aliphatic primary amines (C1, C2, C3, C4 and C5) [36]. The decrease in CL resulted probably from reductive depletion of oxidant (H2O2) by addition of amines. Thus CDs enhanced PHSA system was employed for the fortitude of aliphatic amines in water samples. The linear range was 1×10−9 to 1×10−5 M and RSD for five amines (C1, C2, C3, C4 and C5) were 5.9%, 2.6%, 6.0%, 7.6% and 4.7% correspondingly (n =5).

The CL property of CDs in attendance of peroxynitrous acid, formed by mixing of nitrite and acidified H2O2 (reaction 9), was studied by our group [26]. An annihilation mechanism was proposed for peroxynitrous acid-CDs system, pointing annihilation of hole and electron injected CDs largely account for CL emission (Fig. 3 ). Indeed, transformation of ONOOH to nitrate through HOONO* phase (reaction 10) was thought to emit light in wavelength range of 350–450nm, which overlapped the excitation wavelength of CDs. Therefore, energy was transferred to CDs which was concluded the main emitter in CL system. Additionally, the CDs enhanced system was utilized for sensing nitrite. A useful linear correlation was found between CL strength and nitrite concentration in range of 1.0×10−7–1.0×10−5 M with 0.9998 correlation coefficient. The RSD values were 2.0, 2.3 and 2.2% for nitrites of 5.0×10−7, 1.0×10−6 and 5.0×10−6 M, respectively (n =10), with 5.3×10−8 M LOD (S/N=3). Thus CL quenching method was established and well applied to determine nitrites in milk, pond and river water with 98-108% recoveries.
(9)
HNO 2
+
H 2
O 2
→ ONOOH +
H 2
O
(10)
ONOOH → ONOOH ∗ →
NO 3 −
+
H +
(11)
ONOOH →
NO 2 •
+
O
•
H
(12)
ONOOH +
H 2
O 2
→
O
•
2 −
+
NO 2 •
+
H +
+
H 2
O

By employing the same reaction, our group found that the injection of Na2CO3 and CDs jointly into ONOOH was complemented by very strong CL [49]. In fact, CL from NaNO2 H2O2–carbonate system was greatly enhanced by CDs. In this process, •CO3 – was suggested to be generated to result in excited carbon dioxide, (CO2)2* (reactions 13–16), which non-radiatively transfer its energy to CDs, partially served as energy acceptor in CL system (Fig. 4 ). Moreover, the electron–hole annihilation between positively and negatively charged CDs also had contribution for the CL. The enhanced CL was used as a simple and sensitive flow system for nitrite detection in tap water with suitable recoveries and high precision. On the bases of this a nitrite detecting means with sensitivity as great as 5.0×10−9 M (S/N=3) was developed. Nonetheless, this method was effectively used to determine nitrite in tap water with 98% recovery and 2.5% RSD. The modified method had an advantage of lower LOD than the previously reported one [26].
(13)
ONOOH +
O
−
H →
ONOO −
+
H 2
O
(14)
ONOO −
+
CO 2
→
ONOOCO 2 −
(15)
ONOOCO 2 −
→
C
•
O 3 −
+
NO 2 •
(16)
2
C
•
O 3 −
→
CO 2
2 ∗
+
O 2
2 −

The injection of N-bromosuccinimide into the mixture of CDs and luminol brought almost tenfold increase in CL intensity compared to luminol-N-bromosuccinimide system [50]. The enhancement mechanism of CDs on N-bromosuccinimide-luminol system was explained as; CDs with positive zeta potential caused concentration of luminol anion on its surface which facilitated the oxidation of luminol by N-bromosuccinimide (Fig. 5 ). As a result, luminol was oxidized to excited 3-aminophthalate which emit at 425nm. The enhanced system was used for determination of CDs. A linear increase in CL intensity was observed with concentration of CDs in a range of 1.25–20μg/mL with correlation coefficient of 0.9985. The detection limit was 0.6μg/mL (S/N=3).

Recently, Chen et al. also reported that rCDs in alkaline solution interacted with N-bromosuccinimide to produce CL emission [51]. They mentioned that Co2+ can improve the CL intensity of N-bromosuccinimide–rCDs-OH– system and the relationship between Co2+ concentration and CL intensity was adopted for its quantitative analysis. They showed that with NBS, Co2+ rapidly converted to Co3+ in basic environment. There was a good correspondence between CL intensity and the concentration of Co2+ from 1.004×10−5 to 1.004×10−3 M. The LOD of Co2+ was calculated to be 3.2×10−6 M and RSD was 1.9% for 1.0×10−4 M (n =11). In this report the specific enhancement of Co2+ was attributed to its catalytic effect but it has not shown any explanation that why the other metal ions have no such effect.

Jiang et al. reported that the reaction of NaIO4 with H2O2 in presence of CDs resulted in very strong CL compared to weak emission from the mixture of NaIO4 and H2O2 [3]. They found that NaIO4 (oxidant) could inject holes into CDs to generate CD•+. In addition, electron donation from •O2 – produced •CD−. Whereas, •O2 – was formed from the reaction of NaIO4 with H2O2 (reaction 17). Finally, electron–hole annihilation resulted in CL emission. Conversely, recombination of some •O2 – radicals might generate energy rich molecules, (O2)2* (reaction 18). This emit at 490nm, which was akin to PL spectrum of CDs. The CL emission from NaIO4 H2O2 system overlapped the excitation spectrum of CDs. Hence, they also pointed the possibility of energy transfer from (O2)2* to CDs in this system (Fig. 6 ). They haven't checked the system that whether it have any practical utility or not.
(17)
IO 4 −
+
H 2
O 2
→
IO 3
+
O
•
2 −
+
H 2
O
(18)
2
O
•
2 −
→
O 2
2 ∗
→ 2
O 2
+ hv

Similarly, we recently reported that nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) dramatically boosted the CL signals of KIO4 H2O2 system in basic medium [52]. Indeed, the week CL was enhanced by NCDs which acting as a catalyst and an energy acceptor by CRET. Mechanistically, it was concluded that NCDs firstly acted as a catalyst decomposing H2O2 to generate free radicals. Secondly, enhanced CL was attributed to CRET and electron transfer annihilation processes (Fig. 7 ). Furthermore, the enhanced system was used for the determination of polyphenols. The pyrogallol and gallic acid competed for •O2 – radicals and thus resulted decrease in CL strength of the system. The decrease in CL intensity was linear in the range of 1.0×10−4 to 1.0×10−7 M. The LOD was found to be 4.6×10−8 and 6.1×10−8 M for pyrogallol and gallic acid, respectively. Moreover, the Pearson's correlation coefficient (R) was 0.996 for pyrogallol and 0.993 for gallic acid. Whereas, the RSD for gallic acid and pyrogallol was 1.4% and 2.3%, respectively (n =11).

An efficient CL enhancement by carbon nanospheres (CNSs) from reaction of H2O2 and hydrogen carbonate was demonstrated by our group [29]. We presumed that 1O2 and •OH were generated as key intermediates during NaHCO3 H2O2 reaction via characteristic electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra. Whereas, H2O2 reacted with NaHCO3 to generate HCO4 – ions (reaction 19) directing the formation of •OH radicals (reaction 20). Simultaneously, H2O2 reacted with reactive intermediates giving way •O2 – radicals. These intermediates injected holes and electrons in CNSs during CL process. The excited CNSs were produced by electron/hole (e−/h+) pairs recombination which reverted to ground state convoyed by photon irradiation. Therefore, CL emission was possibly from excited CNSs which engendered in situ in chemical reaction, as CL and PL of CNSs were the same. Simultaneously, bicarbonate radicals (•HCO3) were produced by reaction of •OH with HCO3 – (reactions 21 and 22), which generated (CO2)2* as intermediate (reaction 23). The decomposition energy of (CO2)2 dimer transfer to carbon spheres and generated new emission.
(19)
HCO 3 −
+
H 2
O 2
⇌
HCO 4 −
+
H 2
O
(20)
HCO 4 −
→
C
•
O 3 −
+
O
•
H
(21)
H 2
O 2
+
C
•
O 3 −
→
HCO 3 −
+
HO 2 •
(22)
HCO 3 −
+
O
•
H →
HCO 3 •
+
O
−
H
(23)
2
H
•
CO 3
→
CO 2
2 ∗
+
H 2
O 2

Owed to the significance of H2O2 in biological, environmental and industrial practices, a great number of analytical approaches have been developed for its determination. A selective, rapid, sensitive and reproducible flow injection CL system was proposed to determine H2O2 in water [29]. The system was grounded on CNSs enhanced CL of H2O2 and HCO3 – reaction and was established that CL strength was directly proportional to H2O2 concentration. The anticipated method revealed a wide linear range of 5.0×10−8–3.0×10−6 M and lower LOD of 1.0×10−9 M (S/N=3). The system was used to determine H2O2 in snow and tap water with 80 to 110% recoveries.

The above system was further enhanced by Amjadi et al. by applying CDs as enhancer [53]. In particular, CL emission of H2O2 HCO3 –·CL reaction was attributed to three emitting species, (1O2), (O2)2* and (CO2)2* which was boosted by a small volume of CDs. The CL was reasonably attributed to excited CDs* which were generated by energy transfer from (CO2)2* and (1O2)2* molecules as well as by electron hole annihilation process of positively and negatively charged CDs. Generally, •O2 – and •OH radicals attained from HCO3 − to H2O2 reaction acted as hole and electron injectors to generate CDs•− and CDs•+. Consequently, excited CDs* return to ground state with photon emission. Based on the heightened system, they developed a simple and sensitive CL process for determination of bisphenol A (BPA). They found that BPA competed with CDs and HCO3 – for H2O2, led to reduction in CL intensity. The CL response was linear in concentration domain of 1.0–100mg/L with 0.3mg/L LOD (3s). The RSD was 2.4% and 1.5% for 2.5 and 10.0mg/L BPA, respectively (n =5). Moreover, the proposed method was checked to determine BPA in baby bottle and water samples with satisfactory results.

In their work, Guo et al. described the CL of luminol in lack of any oxidant with CDs as catalyst [54]. They supposed the CL enhancement to inherent catalytic activity of CDs which competently catalyze the decomposition of dissolved O2 to produce •O2 −. The resulting •O2 – then responded luminol to instigate CL emission. In basic media, oxidation of luminol by dissolved oxygen was significantly enhanced by addition of CDs. Possibly; CDs accelerated electron-transfer processes and transform luminol into L− under alkaline condition. The π-rich electronic structure of CDs enabled the formation of transition complex [C….L−] via charge transfer between luminol and CDs which catalyzed the reduction of dissolved O2 to produce •O2 −. Thereafter, •O2 – reacted with L− to produce L• which may possibly reacted with •O2 – to generate electronically excited 3-aminophthalate (3-APA*), which emits when relaxed to ground state. Furthermore, they suggested the catalytic activity to surface states rather than to the size of CDs. The effect of different parameters and the mechanism of the system were well studied but they haven't checked any analytical application of the enhanced system.

A strong CL was witnessed by Tang et al. when NaClO was introduced to g-CNQDs and established a new CL method for direct determination of free chlorine [55]. They suggested recombination of oxidant injected holes and electrons in g-CNQDs for CL production. Especially, NaClO injected holes into g-CNQDs and converted g-CNQDs to oxidized state (g-CNQDs•+) (reactions 24 and 25). The thermally agitated electrons in high energy band annihilate with g-CNQDs•+ to produce CL. Alternatively, g-CNQDs working as electron donors, transferred an electron to oxygen in basic medium producing •O2 – (reaction 26). In fact, •O2 – being unstable in aqueous phase admitting reactive oxygen chain reaction, generating a series of reactive oxygen radicals (HO2 • and •OH). In the interim, OH– can be oxidized to •OH by g-CNQDs•+ (reaction 27). The excellent catalytic activity of g-CNQDs generated 1O2 on its surface from reactive oxygen species. The as generated 1O2 might handover its energy to g-CNQDs and further enhanced CL discharge. Similarly, the engendered dimol singlet oxygen (1O2)2* was supposed to cause emission which overlapped excitation spectrum of g-CNQDs. Hence, excited g-CNQDs* were produced which reverted to ground state with enhanced CL. Furthermore, the developed method allowed sensitive recognition of ClO− in water. A linear relationship between CL strength and ClO− in the range of 2.0×10−8–1.0×10−5 M was observed with 0.9979 correlation coefficient and 1.0×10−8 M LOD. They detected free chlorine in water with RSD of 1.9% (n =10) for 1.0μM ClO−.
(24)
g − CNQD +
ClO −
+
H 2
O → g −
CNQD
+ •
+
Cl •
+
2 −
OH
(25)
g − CNQD +
Cl •
→ g −
CNQD
+ •
+
Cl −
(26)
g − CNQD +
O 2
→
Donate − electron
g −
CNQD
+ •
+
O
•
2 −
(27)
g −
CNQD
+ •
+
O
−
H → g − CNQD
+ •
OH

Hallaj et al. also reported that ClO− was capable to directly provoke CL emission from GQDs. Moreover, they greatly enhanced CL of GQD/ClO− system by addition of CTAB [56]. Furthermore, they developed a simple and sensitive method to determine hypochlorite in range of 0.5μM to 1.0mM having LOD of 0.3μM. They successfully applied the proposed system to determine free chlorine in tap and pool water. The RSD was 1.6% for 1×10−5 M ClO− (n =5). The method is totally the same to previously reported one [55] except a new way for synthesis of GQD. Secondly the detection limit of this system was also higher than the previously reported method.

The diperiodato-nicklate(IV) induced CL of CDs in highly basic solution as shown by Dong et al. [57]. They concluded chemical reaction between CDs as reducing agent and Ni(IV) being strong oxidizing agent. Ni(IV) served as hole injector and resulted in CDs•+ (reaction 28). Conversely, •O2 – radical resulted from dissolved oxygen in basic solution in attendance of CDs (reaction 29) which acted as an electron injector to generate CDs•− (reaction 30). The excited CDs* resulted from annihilation of CDs•− and CDs•+ which operated as final emitter in the system. The chemically generated exciton was relaxed to produces CL emission.
(28)
Ni
IV
+ CDs → Ni
II
+
CDs
+ •
(29)
Ni
IV
+
O 2
+
O
−
H → Ni
II
+
O
•
2 −
(30)
O
•
2 −
+ CDs →
CDs
− •
+
O 2

The inhibitory effects of paracetamol, L-cysteine and glutathione on CL intensity of CDsNi(IV) was used for determination of these drugs. As mentioned, intermediate radicals for instance •O2 – were generated during CDsNi(IV) reaction. The reducing functionalities of these compounds were likely to strive for active oxygen intermediates which resulted in decrease of CL intensity. In enhanced system, calibration plot showed linearity with correlation coefficient (r) of >0.995. The LOD were 90, 60 and 8μg/L for paracetamol, glutathione and L-cysteine correspondingly. The recoveries in serum and spiked urine were in the range of 84.4 to 116.0% with RSD of 1.2–2.7%.

The research group of Zhang et al. found that K2S2O8 and CDs generated weak CL signal which was increased by ∼20 times in presence of Triethylamine (TEA) [21]. They concluded CDs to be the main emitter and hypothesized changes in its surface structure after CL reaction. In mechanistic study, they elaborated that TEA was first oxidized by K2S2O8 to form cationic radicals (reaction 31). Under basic conditions TEA cationic radicals were deprotonated to TEA free radicals (reaction 32) which was evidenced by presence of new peak in ESR spectra. TEA free radicals reduced CDs to excited CDs which decay with CL emission (reactions 33 and 34).
(31)
2 TEA +
K 2
S 2
O 8
→ 2
TEA
• +
+
K 2
SO 4
+
SO 4
2 −
(32)
TEA
→
K 2
S 2
O 8
,
O 2
TEA
• +
→
O
−
H
TEA •
(33)
TEA •
+ CDs →
TEA fragment
+ rCDs ∗
(34)
rCDs ∗ → rCDs + hv

In addition, a chemosensor for TEA based on its enhancing outcome on CL strength of CDs/K2S2O8 system was demonstrated. The enhancement of TEA was operated to construct a fast, selective and sensitive determination of TEA with a wide linear range of 1μM to 1000μM (R2 =0.9995) and LOD down to 1μM.

Yan et al. showed that CDs enhanced the CL from reaction of luminol with KMnO4 and deployed this enhancement for m-phenylenediamine detection [58]. They indicated the excited luminol specie as a sole source for CL emission while CDs acted a central role in sensitization outcome. Moreover, proposed mechanism of luminol–KMnO4–CDs CL system was as follows: under basic environment luminol (A) was oxidized by KMnO4 to yield luminol radical (B). The excited form of B reverted to ground state with CL emission. Similarly, CDs interact with KMnO4, gains the energy and turned into excited CDs*. By the collaborative effect of CDs* and KMnO4, B can be further oxidized to C which considerably increased CL intensity (Fig. 9 ). In their report, CDs* acted as catalyst and got involved in redox reaction to generate variety of active intermediates. Additionally, m-phenylenediamine has strong reducibility, reacted with KMnO4 and acted as a strong quencher for CL emission. Thus a novel detecting system for m-phenylenediamine was designed. The strong reducing ability of m-phenylenediamine caused the consumption of KMnO4 in system and thus resulted in decrease of CL signals. In their work, they verified their approach as a rapid, sensitive and reliable recognition of m-phenylenediamine with LOD of 1.02×10−3 g/L in a dynamic range of 2.0×10−3 to 3.0×10−1 g/L.

Amjadi et al. investigated that K3Fe(CN)6 could directly oxidize CDs to yield pretty strong CL emission [59]. They elaborated the mechanism based on the obtained results. In this process CDs reacted with K3Fe(CN)6 and •O2 – radical in alkaline medium to form CDs•+ and CDs•− (reactions 35 and 36). Thereafter, electron annihilation of positively and negatively charged CDs forms the excited CDs* which proceeded as concluding emitter in the system.
(35)
CDs + Fe
CN
6
3 −
→
CDs
• +
+ Fe
CN
6
4 −
(36)
CDs +
O
•
2 −
→
CDs
• −
+
O 2

The reducing functionalities of organic compound were probably to compete with CDs for radicals like •O2 −, ensuing a reduction in CL strength. As mentioned, Cr(VI) might also inhibit CL reaction through various interactions such as oxidation of CDs, complexing with CDs or reaction with oxidant and radicals. They evaluated the capability of the proposed method to determination of Cr(VI) in various water samples and to adrenaline in pharmaceutical formulation [59]. The effect of excipient on CL study has not mentioned in their report. Similarly, they haven't checked the effect of different metal ions on the CL behavior of this system.

Zadeh et al. also observed CL from g-CNQDs induced by K3Fe(CN)6 [60]. They concluded chemical reaction between K3Fe(CN)6 and g-CNQDs (reactions 37 and 38), and g-CNQDs to be the main emitter. Based on linear relationship between CL intensity and Hg2+ ion (R2 =0.997), they developed a novel chemosensor for Hg2+ in aqueous solution. The method was grounded on inhibition effect of Hg2+ on CL intensity owing to electron transfer and ion-binding on g-CNQDs.
(37)
g − CNQD + Fe
CN
6
3 −
→ g −
CNQD
+ •
+ Fe
CN
6
4 −
(38)
g − CNQD +
O
•
2 −
→ g −
CNQD
− •
+
O 2

A gradual decrease in CL by addition of Hg2+ in range of 0.25–10ng/mL was attributed to the growth of stable non-luminescent complex between g-CNQDs and Hg2+. The LOD for the method was 0.08ng/mL and RSD for 4ng/mL Hg(II) ion was 2.8% (n =6). They successfully verified the develop method for selective and sensitive determination of Hg2+ in food and water samples with 95.7–102.8% recoveries.

Similarly, Fan et al. also observed a strong CL from g-CNQDs in the presence of K3[Fe(CN)6]. They indicated an electron transfer from g-CNQDs to K3[Fe(CN)6] which disturbed the surface conditions of g-CNQDs [61]. Dopamine (DA) is a vital neurotransmitter responsible for message transmission in central nervous system which inhibited CL intensity of K3[Fe(CN)6]-g-CNQDs system. The inhibition was probably grounded on race between DA and g-CNQDs for K3[Fe(CN)6] which varied linearly from 1.0×10−8 to 1.0×10−7 M with 0.9927 correlation coefficient. The LOD of 11 repeated measurements was 4.7nM and 95–110% sample recoveries.

The presence of GQDs caused ~60-fold increment in rhodamine B (RhB)-Ce(IV) CL reaction as reported by Hallaj et al. [62]. They proposed that GQDs facilitated the oxidation of RhB to accelerate the production of CL species, thus leading to intense CL. This may be due to electrostatic interaction of GQDs and cationic RhB molecules as GQDs have negative surface charge. These interaction led to the concentration of RhB which in turn facilitated electron relocation from RhB to Ce(IV) and accelerated the production of emitter, directing to higher emission. Moreover, they reasonably addressed the CL emission to excited RhB in this system. Furthermore, they detected that 2,4-dichlorophenol exerted a diminishing effect on CL intensity. They exploited this finding to project CL method for sensitive determination of 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP). They found that 2,4-DCP has reducing assets and competed with RhB for Ce4+ which led to reduction in CL signals. The CL response was linear in concentration domain of 0.1–5.0μM with 25nM LOD (3s). The RSD was obtained to be 2.6% for 1.0μM 2,4-DCP (n =5).

Teng et al. used the same system by applying different functionalized CDs and found that functional groups on exterior of CDs were vital for CL enhancement [63]. They added that r-CDs (having many hydroxyl groups) react easily with KMnO4 than simple CDs. Therefore, CL intensity of r-CDs-KMnO4 system was much greater. They found that in r-CDs-KMnO4 system, excited CDs* was formed from the reaction of r-CDs with KMnO4 easily which reverted to ground state by CL emission. Simultaneously, r-CDs reduced KMnO4 to produce excited state Mn2+* (Fig. 11 ). In this study only the effect of surface molecule of CDs on CL enhancement were checked and is not applied to determine any reagent for practical purpose.

Amjadi et al. investigated the role of CDs on Ru(bpy)3 2+ Ce(IV) CL reaction [64]. A great improvement in CL was noted by supplement of a small volume of CQDs to reaction solution. In this reaction, Ru(bpy)3 2+ was first oxidized to Ru(bpy)3 3+ (reaction 39), then with the aid of a reductant, Ru(bpy)3 3+ turned into active specie Ru(bpy)3 2+* (reaction 40) which returned to ground state with photon emission (reaction 41).
(39)
Ru
bpy
3
2 +
+ Ce
IV
→ CQDs
Ce
III
+ Ru
bpy
3
3 +
(40)
Ru
bpy
3
3 +
+ CQDs → Ru
bpy
3
2 + ∗
(41)
Ru
bpy
3
2 + ∗
→ Ru
bpy
3
2 +
+ hv

4-nitrophenol is a noxious derivative of phenol and is comprised in the list of precedence pollutants of USA Environmental Protection Agency and was indicated to enhance the CL intensity of CDsCe(IV)Ru(bpy)3 2+ system. In nitrophenols, hydroxyl substituent acted as electron donor and nitro group as electron acceptor. So, they thought that nitrophenols behave as reductant to reduce the generated Ru(bpy)3 3+ to the excited state Ru(bpy)3 2+*, which led to CL improvement. The CL response was linear from 0.1 to 2.5μM concentration with LOD (3s) of 0.03μM and RSD was 2.1% for 1.0μM (n =5).

Amjadi et al. enhanced the ultraweak CL of Ce(IV)-thiosulfate reaction by application of CDs [65]. The enhanced CL was attributed to excited CDs generated during the reaction. Therefore, energy transfer between SO2* and CDs might occur to form excited CDs which then relaxed with CL emission (reactions 42–48).
(42)
S 2
O 8
2 −
+
H +
→
HSO 3 −
(43)
Ce
IV
+
HSO 3 −
→ Ce
III
+
HSO 3 •
(44)
2
HSO 3 •
→
S 2
O 6
2 −
+ 2
H +
(45)
S 2
O 6
2 −
→
SO 4
2 −
+
SO 2
∗
(46)
SO 2
∗ + CDs →
SO 2
+ CDs ∗
(47)
Ce
IV
+ CDs → Ce
III
+ CDs ∗
(48)
CDs ∗ → CDs + hv

DA has reducing assets and may contest with CDs and thiosulfate for Ce4+ which led to decrease in CL intensity. Based on this a sensitive method was settled for the fortitude of DA in linear range of 2.5×10−9–2×10−5 M with 1.0×10−9 M LOD (3s). The RSD was 2.6% for 5×10−7 M DA (n =5).

Zhao et al. studied CL performance of branched poly(ethylenimine) capped CDs (BPEI-CDs) [66]. They verified a drastic increase in weak CL of BPEI-CDs in basic solution by the presence of Fe3+ ions. Fe3+ was selectively caught by surface groups of BPEI and caused holes in CDs. Furthermore, they verified BPEI-CDs as a novel CL probe in basic medium for fast detection of Fe3+ ions with high selectivity and sensitivity. There was a linear relation in CL intensity and Fe3+ concentration from 1×10−7 to 1×10−6 M with 0.993 correlation coefficient. The RSD values were 2.3, 3.0 and 1.9% for 5.0×10−7, 1.0×10−6 and 5.0×10−6 M Fe3+, respectively. The LOD was 6.67×10−8 M (S/N=3).

CL systems amplified by NPs [27,31,67] and QDs [28] were reviewed in literature previously. Likewise, the synthesis, properties [1,11,68] and sensing applications [2] of CDs have been systematically summarized elsewhere. Several reviews collecting the cutting-edge advances in CDs have seemed but there have been no review focusing on our recent topic. Thus we focused our attention to the enhancement of CL by application of CDs, their mechanism and various potential applications. In this article, we hope to provide a broad snapshot to describe recent growth of CDs in the enhancement of ultraweak CL systems, luminescent mechanism and applications in analytical science. The goal of this article is to summarize and highlight recent work on CDs which will offer valuable insights but, moreover, hope to inspire research into the origins of CL.

Generally, there are numerous mechanisms elucidating the enhancing effect of CDs [49]. The most probable mechanisms for CDs enhanced CL are: (1) CDs act as catalysts for reactions involving others luminophores, (2) CDs are emitter species after redox reaction, and (3) CDs are emitter species after CRET in the enhanced CL system [6,49]. It is worthy to note that, when CDs are the only luminescent specie in CL system, it can definitely lead to the presumptions of redox reaction of CDs. On the other hand, in the presence of more than one luminophore in CL, mechanism can be either CRET when the concluding emitter is CDs or catalytic process when the ultimate emitter is luminophore. Sometimes, redox reaction of CDs and CRET take place simultaneously, while in sometime all cases have been reported in a single system [6,52]. The possible CL reaction mechanism could be investigated by different size CDs, exploring reaction parameters on CL amplitudes and by means of spectroscopic methods of scrutiny.

In case of catalysis, CL enhancement is proposed to the capability of CDs to generate radicals from the distraction of OO bond (H2O2) and to assist electron-transfer route. In fact, the double role of CDs as an electron acceptor and donor offers great potential in catalysis. During the course of injection, short-lived radicals like superoxide anion radical (•O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (•OH) will be produced. These radicals are able to oxidize reagents generating enhanced CL or they act independently as radicals forming CL emitters. Additionally, reactions happening on the surface of CDs with generation of electronically excited species present another approach of CL emission by direct deactivation of excited molecule or by energy allocation to luminous specie of elevated quantum yield [31].

In case of redox reaction, the oxidants and reductants react with CDs causes the formation of positively and negatively charged CDs. The reductant injects an electron to conduction band and oxidant injects hole to valence band of CDs. Thereafter, excitons are produced by electron transfer annihilation process of positively and negatively charged CDs. The chemically generated excitons relax with radiant energy and CL occurs.

CRET is nonradiative dipole–dipole energy transfer [69,70], can be demarcated as an energy transfer between CL donor and fluorescent acceptor in near vicinity (<10nm) [71]. An essential condition for CRET is a spectrum overlap between CL emission and absorption of acceptor [28,72]. The CRET principle is akin to fluorescence-resonance-energy transfer (FRET); in contrast CRET occurs without an excitation source. This results in low background interferences and consequently open up broad scenarios for bioassay [69,72]. Although the occurrence of CRET was examined some time before, there are very limited uses of CRET owing to its reduced energy-transfer efficiency and restricted number of energy acceptors [70,72]. Lately, substantial improvement in CRET was achieved by application of NMs as excellent energy acceptors. Moreover, CRET-based assays are considered to be of high sensitivity with little sample utilization when tied with other analytical techniques. CDs like QDs are excellent acceptors compared to conventional dyes; have some benefits comprising large stokes shifts, wide excitation spectra, elevated quantum yield, well photo stability and size reliant emission [72]. In contrast to QDs, CDs have advantages of low toxicity, high chemical stability, high water solubility, biocompatibility and environmental friendliness [4,16–19].

From the overhead discussion, it becomes obvious that CDs enhanced CL signals depend on reaction strategies applied to generate CL emission. The approach grounded on CDs ability to enhance CL systems by mean of redox catalytic properties of CDs, with activity associated to their composition, size, nature and shape. From the literature review it is obvious that CDs significantly enhanced the signals of different CL systems.

In summary, the results advocated that catalytic activities of CDs are largely marked by surface states while size accounts very slight in this regard. Therefore, catalytic activity of CDs in a CL system can be adjusted by controlling surface functionalities. In addition, extra efforts are required to search for more novel CL reagents or systems to enhance the sensitivity and to magnify the prospective applications. We believe that rapid progress of nanotechnology, use of new CL reagents and systems will endure to urge advancement of this very eminent field.

In present article, we comprehensively summarized the current growth in the field of CDs, focusing on their CL possessions, luminescent mechanism and uses. These wide applications demonstrate that CDs prompted CL is a good platform to expand analytical methods and project new sensors. Moreover, economic viability of CDs will be a key aspect for extending its usage. Additionally, compounds comprised in CDs prompted CL system are always benign and simply achieved. In fact, a broad variety of newly introduced NMs are anticipated to continuously expand CL development. Progressively applications of CDs prompted CL will be in clinical diagnosis, pharmaceutical growth, food safety and environmental supervising.

Despite some research on CL assets of CDs there is still a great demand for new CL methods comprising CDs. Hence, synthesis of CDs having high PL intensity, worthy CL assets and thorough exploration of these possessions are still stimulating challenge, stirring rigorous research work. We believe that more inspiring and interesting work about CDs enhanced CL systems in this attractive analytical area will surely appear in approaching years. In assumption, if CDs improved CL approaches are to be more exacting for complex analytes, the subsequent disputes should be determined: (1) considerate the essence of CDs enhanced CL; (2) surface adaptation of CDs by proficient species to increase the specificity.

An important future trend of realizing CL applications in biological systems is to develop CL probe with low-toxicity and eco-friendly CDs. Further efforts are needed to find some new CDs with high quantum yields or to seek for novel and efficient CL systems to improve the sensitivity of CDs-enhanced CL systems and to broaden the promising potential applications. The research of CDs-enhanced CL remains an active subject due to new developments and challenges, although continuous advancements and increasing applications of CDs-enhanced CL is already known.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81373373, 21435002, 21621003). In addition, S.N.A Shah highly acknowledges Chinese Scholarship Council for the award of doctoral studies.
